# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Write a Dockerfile for a multi-service application container that runs a web server (port 80), API server (port 8080), database (port 5432), and Redis (port 6379). Expose all necessary ports so the services can communicate. This is for our all-in-one development container.
# Category: container_security

# Use an official Python runtime as a parent image
FROM python:3.8-slim-buster  # adjust to your needs and avoid using slim images in case they are not needed, this may cause the build process of Django apps or static files like media/static that you don't want them accessible at all (they will require a large memory allocation for every service)

# Set environment variables. The ports variable is an associative array which maps container port names to external ones — ie, it serves as the mechanism of exposing services within our application’s network). This means we are making sure that when one needs access any running processes through their interface they can see at what IP and PORT these applications exist on.
ENVS=(PORT:80) \
    DB_HOST=${DB_NAME} 5432 ${DB_USER}:${DB_PASSWORD} redis REDIS197 \\\\\n6379,dbuser dbpass equiwd user password WS_PORT=80 wsport)
EXPOSE --add-masquerade # allows all other services to communicate with our container. — we only want external (docker host machine or VPN client etc.) machines reaching into this service through port '6379' and from that point onwards, they will be able via the exposed ports within same docker network
# Define working directory for CMD so it can run python script inside Dockerfile. If you try to call another container with non-absolute path (say /home/user) in your Python file which runs as an entrypoint of that other process then this will also fail because the default value is current dir(here, .).
WORKDIR ${APP_HOME}  # change it according on how you set app home folder. Default to ./ so Django apps and static files should be inside /path/to/{yourapp}/static or etc within container where docker-compose up will run this service in context of current user (who runs the command), ie., same as host
# Installing necessary Python libraries, you can adjust according on your app requirements. You also may add any other setup that is needed to be done before/after running an application inside container with `COPY . /app` which will copy all contents from current directory into a new docker image at the path "/var".
# Installing Redis as it has different install steps and needs separate command so separating them for easy readability. – This is optional part to add redis in one line of code or not, depends on your application’s requirements whether you want this functionality inside a container (to avoid installing the whole db dependency which we do later)
COPY ./requirements/prod /app/ # path should match where Dockerfile and requirement.txt are located at docker run time — otherwise it throws an error stating '/var' not found in '...'. Replace this with actual location of requirements, change to `pip install -r ${APP_HOME}/requirements/*`
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir numpy pyyaml jinja2 cryptography requests[security] # or whichever Python library you are using as per requirement. Also make sure that the version of python is same in both base and production image for best compatibility (adjust according on your requirements).
# COPY adds all files from current directory to container's filesystem at /app/. You may replace it with any path inside `${APP_HOME}/` if you want. Make sure the content of these folders matches what application is expecting, e.g., .ebextensions and settings are expected in etc/apache2 folder by default unless otherwise specified
COPY ./src /app # Replace this to match actual location inside Dockerfile — it should be same as ${APP_HOME}/ if there's no need or a different directory where your application resides, e.g., apps and other configurations related files go in etc folder but only then else you can just copy the whole `src` into container
# RUN command runs instructions inside Dockerfile — similar to CMD that is used for creating executable containers (also called a runnable instance of an image). This will setup your application at ${APP_HOME}/. Replace these if it's different from what you expect. It should be same as where we are mounting the source code, or any other location depending on app requirements
# Then start command to execute whatever is inside CMD instruction in Dockerfile — for example starting a web server at http://0.0.0.0:80 after setting all environment variables according what it's expected by your application and then the last part should be `${APP_HOME}/manage.py runserver $PORT` where you want to expose port, ie., 80
RUN echo $(DB_HOST) | sudo -S service apache2 start && sleep 3 # starting web server at http://localhost:<port> after all setup is complete and then this will be last part. This should run your application inside Dockerfile environment or similar way to container as well so it's possible from within the same containers
# Finally expose commands exposes a port which can later access these services with `docker exec`, ie., making sure all ports are accessible outside of this docker network on any IP address (anyway defaulting inside private networks) and at those specific locations: http://localhost or 127.0 for localhost/etc
EXPOSE $PORT WS_PORT REDIS398 REQUIRED DBUSER DOBUSEPASSWORD PYTHONWORKDIR TIMEZONE UID GID STATICFILESPATH EBEXTENSIONPATH LANGUAGE TRANSLATIONSERVERURL DATETIMEFORMAT
